source_metadata.title_raw: Sarah Couch, Chair - I-18-090 - August 23, 2018 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Sarah Couch, Chair 
Capital Fellows Alumni Association 
8900 Sutters Gold Drive 
Sacramento, CA 95826 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-18-090 
Dear Ms. Couch: 
August 23, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Capital Fellows Alumni 
Association ("Association") regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal 
assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an 
opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 
Also, please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby 
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us 
for additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Are food, drinks, and/or entertainment received at events hosted and paid for by the 
Association considered gifts to public officials in attendance? 
2. If such items would be gifts, would they still be considered gifts if the events were open 
to the public? What factors determine whether an event is open to the public? 
3. If an individual or entity sponsors an Association event where food, drink and/or 
entertainment is provided, under what circumstances would they be the source of the gift? 
4. If an individual or entity provides the Association with a general scholarship and the 
Association chooses to spend those funds - without input from the sponsor - to purchase food, 
drink, or entertainment for an event, who is considered the source of the gift? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. 1-18-090 
Page No. 2 
1. Yes. Food, drinks and entertainment would be considered gifts to public officials in 
attendance at Association Events. However, under the drop-in visit exception, if an attendee 
consumes only minimal appetizers and drinks and does not stay for entertainment there is no gift to 
the attendee. 
2. As described, Association Events are geared towards a very narrow and discrete segment 
of the public. Therefore, the events would be gifts to the officials in attendance even if open to the 
public. 
3 and 4. The Association will be the source of the gift unless an individual or entity 
sponsoring the event knows or has reason to know at the time they make their donations which 
public officials, or which identifiable group of public officials, will receive the gifts. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
You are writing on behalf of the Association. The Association serves as the official alumni 
organization for the Capitol Fellows Programs. The programs, which are operated by California 
State University, Sacramento in conjunction with California's Legislative, Judicial, and Executive 
Branches, place fellows at the highest levels of California state government to assist state 
legislators, senior-level executive staff, court administrators with a broad range of public policy 
issues and projects. Capital Fellows and are typically given assignments with a significant amount 
of responsibility and challenge. 
Due to their involvement with policy and decision-making, many fellows are "designated" 
employees in their respective placement's conflict of interest codes. Thus, they are potentially 
subject to the Act's gift limits and reporting requirements. Many of the program's alumni continue 
to work in politics and public service post-fellowship and are also subject to such limits and 
requirements. 
The Association hosts various events, including speaking engagements, occupational 
, seminars, and social events to benefit current fellows, your members, and the Capitol community in 
general. These events are typically open to the public and are advertised via paid online advertising, 
the Association's social media accounts, and word of mouth. All that is distributed at these events, 
if anything, are light appetizers totaling at most two hundred dollars per event. Attendance at events 
depends on a number of factors. However, you typically have on average 40 people at speaking 
engagements, 30 at occupational seminars and 80 at social events. 
The Association pays for these events using funds derived from member dues and a 
fundraiser thrown by the Association several years ago. Member dues are not earmarked for a 
specific purpose. Only one-third of the funds raised at the fundraiser were earmarked for a specific 
purpose, which was funding the Center for California Studies scholarship program, which benefits 
participants in the Capital Fellows Programs. 


File No. I-18-090 
Page No. 3 
The Sacramento State Alumni Association ("SSAA") is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit and the 
Association operates as an affiliate of them. The revenues for the Association are in SSAA's bank 
accounts. 
In an effort to boost fundraising for the Association, the Association's board has considered 
requesting scholarships from individuals, businesses and nonprofits who support the Association's 
goals and mission. It is unclear at this time exactly what the funds derived from scholarships would 
be collected or used for. 
ANALYSIS 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on 
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the 
regular course of business to members o~the public without regard to official status." 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of 
gifts by public officials in three ways: 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is $470 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) 
Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on designated employees requiring that any gift ( or any gifts that 
aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year are disclosed on 
the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 82030 and 87302.) 
Third, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift 
or gifts with an aggregate value of $470 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the 
official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103( e ); 
Regulations 18700 and 18941.) 
Invitation-Only Events 
Generally, a gift is val~ed at its fair market value as of the date of receipt. (Regulation 
18946.) Regulation 18946(d)(5) defines an "Invitation-Only Event" as a gathering of individuals, 
who attend by invitation and where costs are incurred to hold the event beyond the costs of 
providing food. Regulation 18946.2· sets forth an exception to the general gift valuation rule for an 
official at an invitation-only event, and provides in pertinent part as follows: 
"(a) The valuation of attendance at an invitation-only event under this regulation 
applies to the official and one guest of the official attending the event. 
"(b) Invitation-Only Events. Except as provided in subdivisions (d) through (f) of 
this regulation, and in Regulation 18946.4, the admission value of the benefit 
received by an official and one guest who attend an invitation-only event, is the 


File No. 1-18-090 
Page No. 4 
official's and the guest's pro-rata share of the cost of the food, catering services, 
entertainment, and any item provided to the official and guest that is available to 
all guests attending the event. Any other specific benefit provided to the official 
and guest at the event, such as golf green fees, is valued at fair market value. 
"( c) Pro-rata share of the cost of the food, catering services, entertainment, and 
any item provided to the official." The term "pro-rata share" of the cost of the 
food, catering services, entertainment, and any item provided to the official and 
guest" means the cost of all food, catering services, entertainment, and any 
specific item presented to all attendees as part of the event, divided by the number 
of attendees." 
Thus, pursuant to Regulation 18946.2, the value of the benefit received by a public official 
attending one of the Association's events when that event is invitation-only is the pro-rata share of 
the cost of the food, catering services, entertainment, and any item provided to an official that was 
available to all guests in attendance. Any other personal benefit provided to an official and his or 
her guest and would be valued at its fair-market value. 
Drop-In Visit Exception/or In~itation Only Events 
lfan official attends an invitation-only event and does not consume any meal or stay for any 
entertainment and consumes only minimal appetizers and drinks, the value of the gift received is the 
value of any specific item, other than food, presented to the official and guest accompanying the 
official at the event.2 (Regulation 18946.2(e).) 
Rebates or Discounts in the Price of Anything of Value Made on the Regular Course of Business 
to Members of the Public Without Regard to Official Status. 
You ask if the events being open to the public would change the analysis. While there is an 
exception for rebates or discounts in the price of anything of value made in the regular course of 
business to members of the public without regard to official status (Section 82028(a)), this 
exception does not generally apply when an event is geared towards a very narrow and discrete 
segment. (Jacobs Advice Letter, No. A-14-211). The events you have asked about appear to be 
geared toward a very narrow and tliscrete segment of the public, as well as a correspondingly 
discrete segment of public officials: current fellows, the Association's members and the "Capitol 
community." This discount would not be offered to a diverse or heterogeneous assortment of 
individuals. Thus, the food, catering and entertainment offered at the events would not be 
considered a discount made available to members of the public without regard to their official 
status. 
2 For purposes of Regulation 18946.2, "entertainment" means a feature show or performance intended for an 
audience, and does not include music provided for background ambiance. Additionally, in the case that a lobbyist, 
lobbying firm, or lobbyist employer sponsors an event please refer to Regulation 18640 for gift valuation. 


Events that Have Been Sponsored by Outside Individuals or Entities 
File No. I-18-090 
Page No. 5 
You have asked if the Association's events are sponsored by certain groups or individuals if 
it is possible that those individuals or groups would be the source of the gift. 
Regulation 18945 provides the Commission's rule for determining the source of a gift and 
provides the following, in pertinent part: 
"(a) The person who makes the gift to the official(s) is the source of the 
gift unless that person is acting as an intermediary. The person is acting as 
an intermediary for the source of the gift when the gift to the official was 
provided under any of the following conditions: 
"(1) The person receives a payment from a source and the payment is 
made to the official after the source identifies the official as the intended 
recipient of the gift; 
"(2) The person receives a payment from a source after soliciting the 
payment with the understanding that the payment will be used for the sole 
or primary purpose of making a gift to an official; or 
"(3) The person receives a payment from a source after the payment was 
solicited by the official or the official's agent for the purpose of making a 
gift to the official. 
"(b) Under any of the conditions identified in subdivision (a)(l)-(3), the 
source of the payment is the source of the gift. 
"(c) If a public official's pro-rata share of the cost of the benefit provided 
at an event constitutes a gift to the official, the person hosting the event, 
unless the admission to the event was provided by someone other than the 
host, shall be deemed the source of the gift so long as the event is widely 
attended by persons other than governmental officials. 
"(d) Presumption of Source by Official. Notwithstanding subdivision (a), 
an official may presume that the person delivering the gift or, if the gift is 
offered but has not been delivered, the person offering the gift to him or 
her is the source of the gift unless it is clear from the surrounding 
circumstances at the time the gift is delivered or offered that the person 
delivering or offering the gift is not the actual source of the gift. 
"( e) Presumption of Source by Intermediaries. A person that qualifies as 
an intermediary as a result of a payment solicited from an official pursuant 
to subdivision (a)(3) may presume that he, she, or it is the source of the 
gift unless the person does not know or have reason to know of the 
official's solicitation." 


File No. I-18-090 
Page No. 6 
If the Association's sponsors know or have reason to know at the time they make their 
donations which public officials, or which identifiable group of public officials will receive the gifts 
the sponsors are the true source of the gift. (McCloskey Advice Letter, No. 1-91-263 and 
Hollingsworth Advice Letter, No. A-10-133.) For example, if the event is one that any legislator or 
public official may be invited to attend, and there is no past pattern to indicate that a certain 
legislator or selective group will be invited, the sponsor would not know or have reason to know 
which public officials or which identifiable group of public officials will receive the gifts. We do 
not have sufficient facts to determine whether the sponsors have reason to know who will be 
invited. However, if they do, the sponsors will be the true source of the gifts to the public officials. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
Addiuf<- .~ 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on 
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the 
regular course of business to members o~the public without regard to official status." 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of 
gifts by public officials in three ways: 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is $470 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) 
Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on designated employees requiring that any gift ( or any gifts that 
aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year are disclosed on 
the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 82030 and 87302.) 
Third, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift 
or gifts with an aggregate value of $470 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the 
official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103( e );

Generally, a gift is val~ed at its fair market value as of the date of receipt. (Regulation

who attend by invitation and where costs are incurred to hold the event beyond the costs of

"(a) The valuation of attendance at an invitation-only event under this regulation 
applies to the official and one guest of the official attending the event. 
"(b) Invitation-Only Events. Except as provided in subdivisions (d) through (f) of

received by an official and one guest who attend an invitation-only event, is the

entertainment, and any item provided to the official and guest that is available to 
all guests attending the event. Any other specific benefit provided to the official 
and guest at the event, such as golf green fees, is valued at fair market value. 
"( c) Pro-rata share of the cost of the food, catering services, entertainment, and 
any item provided to the official." The term "pro-rata share" of the cost of the 
food, catering services, entertainment, and any item provided to the official and 
guest" means the cost of all food, catering services, entertainment, and any 
specific item presented to all attendees as part of the event, divided by the number 
of attendees." 
Thus, pursuant to Regulation 18946.2, the value of the benefit received by a public official 
attending one of the Association's events when that event is invitation-only is the pro-rata share of 
the cost of the food, catering services, entertainment, and any item provided to an official that was 
available to all guests in attendance. Any other personal benefit provided to an official and his or 
her guest and would be valued at its fair-market value. 
Drop-In Visit Exception/or In~itation Only Events 
lfan official attends an invitation-only event and does not consume any meal or stay for any 
entertainment and consumes only minimal appetizers and drinks, the value of the gift received is the 
value of any specific item, other than food, presented to the official and guest accompanying the 
official at the event.2 (Regulation 18946.2(e).) 
Rebates or Discounts in the Price of Anything of Value Made on the Regular Course of Business 
to Members of the Public Without Regard to Official Status. 
You ask if the events being open to the public would change the analysis. While there is an 
exception for rebates or discounts in the price of anything of value made in the regular course of 
business to members of the public without regard to official status (Section 82028(a)), this 
exception does not generally apply when an event is geared towards a very narrow and discrete 
segment. (Jacobs Advice Letter, No. A-14-211). The events you have asked about appear to be 
geared toward a very narrow and tliscrete segment of the public, as well as a correspondingly 
discrete segment of public officials: current fellows, the Association's members and the "Capitol 
community." This discount would not be offered to a diverse or heterogeneous assortment of 
individuals. Thus, the food, catering and entertainment offered at the events would not be 
considered a discount made available to members of the public without regard to their official 
status. 
2 For purposes of Regulation 18946.2, "entertainment" means a feature show or performance intended for an 
audience, and does not include music provided for background ambiance. Additionally, in the case that a lobbyist, 
lobbying firm, or lobbyist employer sponsors an event please refer to Regulation 18640 for gift valuation. 


Events that Have Been Sponsored by Outside Individuals or Entities  
You have asked if the Association's events are sponsored by certain groups or individuals if 
it is possible that those individuals or groups would be the source of the gift. 
Regulation 18945 provides the Commission's rule for determining the source of a gift and 
provides the following, in pertinent part: 
"(a) The person who makes the gift to the official(s) is the source of the 
gift unless that person is acting as an intermediary. The person is acting as 
an intermediary for the source of the gift when the gift to the official was 
provided under any of the following conditions: 
"(1) The person receives a payment from a source and the payment is 
made to the official after the source identifies the official as the intended 
recipient of the gift; 
"(2) The person receives a payment from a source after soliciting the 
payment with the understanding that the payment will be used for the sole 
or primary purpose of making a gift to an official; or 
"(3) The person receives a payment from a source after the payment was 
solicited by the official or the official's agent for the purpose of making a 
gift to the official. 
"(b) Under any of the conditions identified in subdivision (a)(l)-(3), the

not have sufficient facts to determine whether the sponsors have reason to know who will be 
invited. However, if they do, the sponsors will be the true source of the gifts to the public officials.
